CHICHA San Chen Black Tea Honey

chinese: 凤眉红茶 + 百花香蜜

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general black tea beverage class; no specific honey-sweetened black tea entry exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers black tea infusion as closest parent; honey addition is not separately coded. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 227613004 covers black tea as a substance; honey-sweetened variant not separately coded. Closest parent used.

main_ingredients: black tea leaves (Feng Mei / Phoenix Eyebrow grade)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 300 ml (300 g) of brewed black tea sweetened with approximately 15–20 g of honey, as typically served in a Taiwanese-style tea shop.

nutrient_source.title: Tea, black, brewed, prepared with tap water (USDA SR Legacy #14355) and Honey (USDA SR Legacy #19296) — combined estimation
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for honey-sweetened black tea exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Values are estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy data for brewed black tea (FDC #14355) and honey (FDC #19296) in a ratio reflecting approximately 95 g brewed tea + 5 g honey per 100 g of beverage. This ratio is consistent with a 15–20 g honey addition to a 300 ml serving. Energy, carbohydrate, and sugar are primarily driven by the honey fraction; protein, fat, and fibre are effectively zero from both components.

gi_source.title: Honey, GI value from University of Sydney GI Database; black tea base contributes negligible glycemic load
gi_source.source_match_notes: Plain brewed black tea has a GI of approximately 0–1 (no glycemic carbohydrates). Honey has a published GI of approximately 55 (low) per the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care). The GI of this beverage is estimated at 55 (low) because the glycemic response is driven entirely by the honey fraction. Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (55 × 12.9 g available carbohydrate) / 100 ≈ 7.1, rounded to 7.0. Available carbohydrate per serving = 4.3 g per 100 g × 3 = 12.9 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (~9 mg per 300 ml serving); black tea polyphenols may modestly support vascular function
hypertension.guidance: Black tea with honey is low in sodium and suitable for individuals managing hypertension; regular unsweetened black tea is preferable to minimise added sugar intake, but a moderate honey addition poses minimal blood pressure risk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Honey contributes free sugars (~12–15 g per serving) with a low glycemic index of approximately 55, which can still raise postprandial blood glucose due to the sugar content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit or avoid honey-sweetened tea due to the free sugar content; if consumed, portion size should be restricted and blood glucose monitored, as the honey fraction contributes a glycemic load of approximately 7 per standard serving, despite the beverage having a low GI of 55.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat and cholesterol; black tea flavonoids (theaflavins, thearubigins) are associated with modest cardioprotective effects in epidemiological studies
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Black tea with honey is very low in saturated fat and cholesterol, making it broadly compatible with a heart-healthy diet; however, the added honey contributes free sugars that should be accounted for within daily energy and sugar targets for cardiovascular risk management.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Black Tea with Honey (蜂蜜红茶) is a sweetened hot or cold tea beverage made by brewing high-grade black tea leaves — in this instance the Feng Mei (凤眉, Phoenix Eyebrow) cultivar — and sweetening the infusion with polyfloral honey (百花香蜜, Bai Hua Xiang Mi). The beverage is served at CHICHA San Chen, a Taiwanese specialty tea brand with an outlet at One Fullerton, Singapore. The tea base is described as heavily roasted, producing a mellow, full-bodied flavour with natural sweetness and a crisp, clean finish. Honey adds floral sweetness and a smooth mouthfeel without the sharpness of refined sugar. Nutritionally, the beverage is very low in energy (approximately 17 kcal per 100 g; ~51 kcal per 300 ml serving), contains negligible protein and fat, and derives its carbohydrate content almost entirely from the honey fraction (approximately 4.3 g carbohydrate per 100 g, predominantly as fructose and glucose). Dietary fibre is effectively zero. Sodium is very low (~3 mg per 100 g). Black tea polyphenols — particularly theaflavins and thearubigins — are present and have been associated with antioxidant and modest cardioprotective effects in epidemiological research. The glycemic index is estimated at 55 (low), driven by the honey component; the glycemic load per standard 300 ml serving is approximately 7. Clinically, this beverage is low-risk for hypertension and cardiovascular disease but warrants caution for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to the free sugar content from honey. It is vegetarian, halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free, but not vegan due to the honey ingredient.

confidence_reason: No direct nutritional entry for honey-sweetened black tea exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central as a single composite food. Values are estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy data for brewed black tea and honey in a ratio consistent with the described serving. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database honey entry (GI ~55). Confidence is medium rather than low because both component sources are approved primary sources and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy FDC #14355 (brewed black tea) and FDC #19296 (honey) at approximately 95:5 ratio per 100 g of beverage. 2. fat_g is naturally zero for the brewed black tea component; the honey component also contributes no fat. Confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for both components. 3. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, and cholesterol_mg are confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for both brewed black tea and honey. 4. dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero for a brewed tea beverage; tea leaves are not consumed. 5. glycemic_index set to 55 (low) based on honey GI from University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al., 2008); plain black tea contributes no glycemic carbohydrates. gi_category is low. glycemic_load_per_serving calculated as (55 × 12.9) / 100 = 7.1, rounded to 7.0 for a 300 g serving. 6. The following micronutrients are set to -1 because they are not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source (FDC #14355 brewed black tea and FDC #19296 honey) at the precision required for this composite estimate, and therefore cannot be reliably derived: vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for brewed black tea or honey. vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for brewed black tea or honey. vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for brewed black tea or honey. vitamin_b3_niacin_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for brewed black tea or honey. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for brewed black tea or honey. vitamin_b12_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for brewed black tea or honey; black tea and honey contain no vitamin B12 by nature. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for brewed black tea or honey. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for brewed black tea or honey; vitamin D is naturally zero in plant-based tea and honey. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for brewed black tea or honey. zinc_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for brewed black tea or honey. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for brewed black tea or honey. 7. The food_name in the input ('Black Tea + Honey凤眉红茶 + 百花香蜜') has been normalised to the canonical English form 'Black Tea with Honey'; the original Chinese name 凤眉红茶 + 百花香蜜 is preserved in aliases. 8. This beverage is not vegan due to the honey ingredient. 9. Multilingual names follow Singaporean dish rules (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) as the food is served in Singapore. 10. Ontology codes use closest_parent match type throughout as no exact composite honey-sweetened black tea entry exists in FoodOn, FoodEx2, or SNOMED CT.
